## Summary

**Objective.** Decide what, if anything, our orchestrator should borrow from
**Odysseus** — Felix Kjellberg's MIT-licensed self-hosted AI workspace
(`github.com/pewdiepie-archdaemon/odysseus`, released 2026-05-31) — by comparing
the two designs at the four seams they share. **Finding.** The projects sit at
opposite ends of the model-capability axis: Odysseus is a FastAPI product shell
tuned for dumb local models, ours is an orchestrator spine driving capable
frontier CLIs over ConPTY for subscription billing. That single difference
explains nearly every divergence. Two patterns are worth adopting as conventions
(flat `detect → build → parse` provider dispatch; model-declared completion with
tool-results-as-messages); the rest of Odysseus is product surface to leave
alone, and its omission of an interactive-approval layer is external validation
of our Interactivity axis rather than a gap to close.

## Questions

### Should we borrow Odysseus's provider abstraction?

Both projects must abstract over multiple model backends. Does Odysseus's
approach suggest a better shape than our current CLI-substrate seam
([ADR 0004](../design/adr/0004-provider-seam.md))?

### Is there anything in their agent loop / tool dispatch we should copy?

Odysseus owns a tool-dispatch loop; we delegate the turn to `claude`/`codex`. Do
their loop mechanics (tool-calling, completion contract) carry over to our Steps
and step-completion semantics?

### How does their interactivity & permission model compare to ours?

Our permission-interaction model splits a Permission gate from an Interaction
intercom on one resolve seam. Did a 30k-star project converge on the same axis,
or skip it — and what does that tell us about whether the axis is real?

### Do they have a memory capability we lack?

Odysseus ships an in-product vector memory store. Is its absence from our design
a gap, or a deliberate consequence of being a different category of product?

## Expected Result

A high-profile, similarly-named project was expected to be either a near-duplicate
worth heavy borrowing, or wholly unrelated. The prior lean was that little would
transfer, because our thesis (frontier CLIs under subscription billing) is narrow
and unusual — but with no strong read on whether any concrete pattern would survive
the comparison.

## Quotations

### Completion is model-declared, not timed
source: src/agent_loop.py (paraphrased design note)

"The agent declares when the job is done… Never trail off mid-task." Three exits
are defined: success, blocked (capability or permission missing, stated plainly),
and continuation.

### Tools execute immediately upon detection
source: Odysseus README / agent_loop.py

"Tools execute immediately upon detection." There is no approval step — the loop
is autonomous-only, and the only access control is RBAC over which tools a user
may hold.

### Dual tool-calling for weak backends
source: src/agent_loop.py

Native OpenAI function-calling is used when the endpoint supports it, plus a
fallback where the model emits a fenced code block with the tool name as the
language tag and the block auto-executes — so small local models that cannot do
structured tool-calls still drive tools.

## Analysis

**Category and overlap.** Odysseus is a product/UI shell (Python 3.11 ·
FastAPI/Uvicorn · SQLite · ChromaDB · Docker Compose with SearXNG + ntfy) that
talks to model HTTP endpoints, explicitly tuned for small local models. Ours is
an orchestrator spine driving coding-agent CLIs over ConPTY. Most of Odysseus —
Cookbook, email/calendar, image-gen, RBAC, the vanilla-JS frontend — is product
surface with no analog here. The comparison reduces to four shared seams:
provider abstraction, agent loop / tool dispatch, interactivity & permission, and
memory.

**Provider abstraction.** `src/llm_core.py` detects the provider by hostname
match on the endpoint URL (`_detect_provider()` →
`anthropic|ollama|openai|openrouter|groq|copilot`), then routes to per-provider
payload builders and response parsers, with a `_sanitize_llm_messages()` pass
that strips app-only metadata before sending; per-model quirks (o1/o3 rejecting
temperature, `max_completion_tokens`, Ollama's `num_ctx`) are handled inline.
Theirs abstracts JSON-over-REST shapes while ours abstracts CLI drive substrates
(PTY/subprocess + transcript parse) — a different problem — but the
**detect → build → parse triple with zero inheritance** is the same flat shape our
seam already uses, and it is proven to scale to six providers. That is a useful
reference point if we ever add an API-key provider next to the CLI ones.

**Agent loop / tool dispatch.** `src/agent_loop.py` runs a capped loop
(`MAX_AGENT_ROUNDS`): generate → `parse_tool_blocks()` → `execute_tool_block()`
(60s timeout, 10K-char output cap) → results injected back as user-role
`[Tool execution results]` messages → loop. Two design choices stand out. The
**dual tool-calling** fallback (quotation, id 9) exists specifically so weak local
models still drive tools. And **completion is model-declared, not timed**
(quotation, id 7), with a three-exit contract. We do not own a tool-dispatch loop —
`claude`/`codex` run their own turn internally — so most of this is not ours to
copy. But the completion contract and the tool-result-as-message pattern address
the same problems our Steps and step-completion semantics will face; the
"state the blocker plainly, never trail off" rule in particular maps directly to
our "throw actionable errors, never swallow them silently" standard.

**Interactivity & permission.** Odysseus is autonomous-only (quotation, id 8): its
"permission model" is RBAC, not action-gating — non-admin users simply lack
shell/Python/file tools, admin-only covers MCP management, tokens, and webhooks.
Coarse, static, per-user. Our
[permission-interaction-model](../PLANS/permission-interaction-model.md) splits two
concerns on one resolve seam — a Permission gate plus an Interaction intercom —
with `Interactivity {Interactive, Autonomous}`, `IDecisionResolver`, and the
structured-questions spike. Odysseus picked the Autonomous half and skipped the
Interactive half entirely. A 30k-star project omitting interactive approval is a
clean foil: it shows the Interactive/Autonomous axis is a real design dimension,
deliberately chosen, not over-engineering.

**Memory.** Odysseus has a ChromaDB vector + keyword retrieval store that "evolves
over time." Ours is operator-side file memory for the agent driving the repo, not
an in-product agent memory store. Not a gap — a different product.

**Adjacent, for completeness.** Their Deep Research feature was *ported* from
Alibaba's DeepResearch (gather → read → synthesize → visual report) rather than
built — a reminder that lifting a proven harness can beat building one. Their
Cookbook / `hwfit` does VRAM-aware model recommendation across 270+ models, with
no analog here because the CLI subscription, not us, picks the model.

## Outcome

The expectation that little would transfer held — but the comparison surfaced two
concrete patterns worth keeping, sharpening the vague prior into actionable
guidance. Answering the questions:

- **Provider abstraction (id 2).** Adopt their flat `detect → build → parse`
  shape as our provider-seam convention when a second API provider lands; keep the
  seam flat and resist a provider base class. Aligns with YAGNI / least-mechanism.
- **Agent loop (id 3).** Don't copy the loop. Do capture "model-declared
  completion + tool-result-as-message" as oracle/step guidance, citing their
  three-exit contract as prior art when we settle step-completion semantics. Keep
  the fenced-block tool fallback in our back pocket only if we ever broaden past
  `claude`/`codex` to a weak/local backend.
- **Interactivity & permission (id 4).** Nothing to borrow; this is our clearest
  advantage. Use Odysseus as external validation of the Interactivity axis in the
  PRD/ADR narrative — a project that omitted interactive approval shows the
  dimension is real and deliberately chosen.
- **Memory (id 5).** No gap. Their in-product store is a different category;
  leave it.

Net: borrow the flat provider dispatch and the completion contract, take their
missing interactivity layer as validation of ours, and leave the rest. Their
entire product surface — Cookbook, email/calendar, image-gen, in-product memory,
RBAC — is an explicit non-goal; resist scope creep toward "workspace."

## Open Questions

### Where should the "model-declared completion" prior art be recorded?
lean: Cite it in the oracle/step-completion notes when that work lands, not as a standalone doc now.

The three-exit completion contract is worth citing, but only at the moment we
firm up step-completion semantics. Options: (a) drop a forward-reference note now
against the relevant oracle item; (b) wait and cite it inline when the work
lands. Deferring avoids a dangling note, but risks losing the reference.

### Do we pre-place a note for the fenced-block tool fallback?
lean: Note it against the Ollama row in agent-providers.md, but build nothing.

The language-tag-as-tool-name fallback is the known-good pattern for backends
that can't do structured tool-calls, but we don't drive any such backend today.
Options: (a) record it now against the Ollama row in
[agent-providers.md](../research/agent-providers.md) as a back-pocket pattern;
(b) leave it entirely until we actually broaden past `claude`/`codex`.
